skill.name: lark-attendance
skill.description: Query your own Lark attendance check-in records through a CLI interface. Check work schedules, overtime, and leave status.
skill.summary: Query your own Lark attendance check-in records
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: All 28 static findings are false positives. The external_commands flags (25) are markdown code fences in SKILL.md, not executable code. The filesystem/path_traversal flags (2) at line 13 are legitimate relative paths to shared authentication code within the monorepo. The crypto flag is a false match on the word 'description' in YAML frontmatter. This is a read-only attendance query skill using the official lark-cli tool.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Shell Command Pattern in Markdown
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged markdown code fences (```bash) as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are legitimate documentation code blocks, not executable code. The skill uses the lark-cli binary, not inline shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations contain markdown code fences (```bash) used for documentation purposes. The skill does not execute any shell commands directly.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Path Traversal in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged '../lark-shared/SKILL.md' as path traversal. This is a legitimate relative path reference to shared authentication code within the same monorepo repository structure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path reference points to a local skill file within the same repository. No user input can influence this path, and there is no file access vulnerability.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 'description' (containing 'MD' substring) as weak cryptographic algorithm. This is a false match on the YAML frontmatter word.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is 'description:' in YAML frontmatter. No cryptographic operations or algorithms are present in this skill.

content.user_title: Query Your Lark Attendance Records Instantly
content.value_statement: HR teams waste hours manually checking attendance records. This skill lets Claude query your Lark attendance data through a simple CLI, displaying check-in times, overtime, and leave status in natural language.
actual_capabilities: Query personal attendance check-in and check-out records
View work schedules and assigned shifts
Check overtime hours and compensatory time
Review leave status and approved time off
Access attendance statistics for date ranges
Display results in human-readable format via CLI
limitations: Only queries your own attendance data (no admin bulk export)
Requires lark-cli binary to be installed locally
Cannot modify or submit attendance records, read-only access

use_cases.title: Employee Self-Service
use_cases.description: Employees quickly check their own attendance records, overtime balances, and leave status without logging into the Lark web portal.
use_cases.target_user: Individual employees tracking their work hours

use_cases.title: HR Support Teams
use_cases.description: HR staff help employees verify attendance data when resolving payroll discrepancies or leave disputes.

use_cases.title: Team Leads Review
use_cases.description: Team leads check their direct reports' attendance patterns to prepare for performance reviews or scheduling discussions.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Attendance This Week
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the lark-attendance skill to query my check-in records for this week
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick daily check to verify all days were recorded

prompt_templates.title: Get Overtime Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Check my overtime hours for the past month using the attendance skill

prompt_templates.title: Review Leave Balance
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my attendance record and any approved leave for March

prompt_templates.title: Full Attendance Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Query my complete attendance data for Q1 including overtime and leave, format as a summary

output_examples.output:

output_examples.output:

best_practices: Always verify the lark-cli tool is installed and authenticated before querying attendance
Specify date ranges clearly to avoid pulling excessive historical data
Review the lark-cli schema output before running queries to ensure correct parameter structure
anti_patterns: Do not attempt to modify or submit attendance records through this skill (read-only)
Avoid querying other employees' data without proper authorization
Do not use this skill to generate attendance reports for payroll without HR approval

faq.question: Does this skill store my attendance data?
faq.answer: No. This skill only queries data from Lark via the lark-cli tool. No data is stored locally.

faq.question: Can I see my colleagues' attendance records?
faq.answer: No. This skill is designed for querying your own attendance data only. Viewing others' records requires admin permissions.

faq.question: What happens if my check-in was missed?
faq.answer: The skill will show the attendance record as-is from Lark. Contact HR to resolve missing check-ins.

faq.question: Do I need special permissions to use this skill?
faq.answer: You need a valid Lark account with the attendance:task:readonly scope. The lark-cli handles authentication.

faq.question: Can I export attendance data to a spreadsheet?
faq.answer: This skill displays data in natural language. For spreadsheet exports, ask Claude to format the output as CSV.

faq.question: Why does the skill require reading lark-shared first?
faq.answer: The lark-shared file contains shared authentication and permission handling code used by all Lark skills.
